passages.text: Nonlinear association between the erythrocyte distribution width-to-albumin ratio and mortality in older adults: A retrospective cohort study using NHANES 1999 to 2018 data

Older adults face high risks of all-cause and cardiovascular disease (CVD) mortality; however, simple, inexpensive biomarkers for early risk identification are limited. The red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio (RAR) is a low-cost, routinely measured marker, but its prognostic value in older adults is unclear. We examined whether RAR is independently associated with all-cause and CVD mortality in older adults. We conducted a retrospective cohort study using data from the United States National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (1999–2018). The study included 16,558 adults aged ≥60 years, with median follow-up of 9.4 years. Multivariable Cox proportional hazards models estimated associations between RAR and mortality. Nonlinearity was evaluated using generalized additive models with penalized splines and two-piecewise Cox models combined with a recursive algorithm. Among 16,558 participants (mean age 70.7 ± 7.3 years; 49.8% men), RAR quartiles were Q1 (2.32–2.95), Q2 (2.95–3.16), Q3 (3.16–3.45), and Q4 (>3.45). Over a median 9.4-year follow-up, 6119 deaths occurred, including 2048 CVD deaths. In fully adjusted models, RAR was associated with greater risks of all-cause mortality (hazard ratio = 2.15, 95% confidence interval = 1.89–2.45) and CVD mortality (hazard ratio = 2.05, 95% confidence interval = 1.74–2.41). Associations were nonlinear, with a threshold at RAR = 4.05; below this level, higher RAR was linked to increased all-cause and CVD mortality, whereas above it, the association weakened and CVD mortality risk plateaued. RAR was independently and nonlinearly associated with all-cause and CVD mortality in older adults. These findings support RAR as a simple biomarker for mortality risk identification in older adults.
1. Introduction

The global population is aging rapidly, with projections indicating that more than 2.1 billion individuals will be aged 60 years or older by 2050. As of 2024, the elderly population in the United States (U.S.) comprises approximately 61.2 million individuals, representing 18% of the nation’s total population. Older adults experience higher mortality rates, primarily attributable to age-related physiological decline, a substantial burden of chronic diseases, cognitive impairment, and nutritional or metabolic imbalances. Although various biomarkers – serum neurofilament light chain (sNfL), interleukin-6, high-sensitivity C-reactive protein, N-terminal pro–B-type natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP), and cystatin C have been investigated for mortality risk assessment in older adults, few are widely applied clinically. Biomarkers such as sNfL and NT-proBNP necessitate the use of costly specialized immunoassay platforms, thereby imposing significant financial burdens on healthcare systems. Furthermore, sNfL lacks standardized testing protocols, while measurements of interleukin-6 and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein are susceptible to fluctuations due to acute illnesses. NT-proBNP is characterized by considerable age and sex-related variability without established reference ranges, and cystatin C exhibits notable interlaboratory variability and method-dependent bias, lacking consistent mortality cutoffs. Consequently, these biomarkers are limited by low specificity, lack of standardization, and high costs. There is an urgent need for simple, affordable blood tests to identify high mortality risk in older adults in resource-limited primary care.

Red cell distribution width (RDW) is a standard hematological parameter that reflects variability in erythrocyte volume. Elevated RDW values may result from the effects of pro-inflammatory cytokines, which impair erythrocyte maturation, shorten erythrocyte lifespan, and disrupt iron metabolism and erythropoietin synthesis. RDW serves as an indicator of systemic inflammation and oxidative stress and has been associated with the development and prognosis of several conditions, including malignant neoplasms, hepatic disorders, and cardiovascular disease (CVD).

Serum albumin is a biomarker of nutritional status and systemic inflammation. It contributes to multiple physiological processes, including antioxidant defense, detoxification, immune modulation, and attenuation of inflammatory responses. Lower serum albumin concentrations have been associated with increased risk of age-related diseases and higher mortality rates. In contrast, RDW has been observed to increase with advancing age. Together, RDW and serum albumin provide complementary insights into the biological processes underlying aging and age-related morbidity.

RDW and serum albumin are routinely assessed laboratory biomarkers obtained from standard hematological and biochemical testing platforms, which are universally accessible in clinical laboratories and do not necessitate additional specimens or reagents. In contrast to biomarkers such as sNfL and NT-proBNP, which require expensive specialized immunoassay platforms, the measurement of RDW and albumin incurs no additional cost. These attributes make RDW and albumin a practical and cost-effective option for health monitoring and mortality risk assessment, particularly in resource-limited primary care settings and low-to-middle-income countries where specialized immunoassay platforms may be inaccessible or economically prohibitive.

Previous research has established the red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio (RAR) as a significant biomarker across various populations, predominantly involving adults aged 18 years and older. This biomarker has been linked with conditions such as asthma, cerebrovascular disease, hypertension, and coronary artery disease, where it correlates with disease progression and adverse clinical outcomes. Additionally, RAR has been associated with short-term and in-hospital mortality in acute conditions, including acute respiratory distress syndrome and sepsis, particularly among critically ill patients. Furthermore, studies have identified correlations between RAR and all-cause mortality in both the general population and individuals with chronic conditions like diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Nonetheless, previous investigations have largely focused on younger or mixed-age cohorts, leaving the independent association between RAR and mortality risk in community-dwelling older adults (aged 60 years and above), especially over extended follow-up periods, inadequately explored.

To address this knowledge gap, data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) were examined to evaluate the association between the RAR and the risk of all-cause and CVD mortality in older adults.
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Study population

The NHANES is a cross-sectional program designed to collect nationally representative health-related data from the noninstitutionalized civilian population of the U.S. NHANES enrolls civilian, noninstitutionalized U.S. residents of all ages living in households across all 50 states and the District of Columbia, while excluding individuals residing in institutions (such as nursing homes, long-term care facilities, and prisons) and active-duty military personnel. Since 1999, NHANES has collected a wide range of information, including demographic characteristics, dietary intake, physical examination results, laboratory test findings, questionnaire data, and mortality records linked to the National Death Index. The NHANES protocols were approved by the National Center for Health Statistics Research Ethics Review Board, and all participants provided written informed consent. Because the present study used publicly available, de-identified NHANES data, institutional review board approval for this secondary analysis was not required. NHANES data are publicly accessible (https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/). The study was conducted in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology guidelines for cohort studies.

For the present retrospective cohort study, NHANES data from 1999 to 2018 were utilized, with a total of 101,316 participants across cycles (1999–2000, N = 9965; 2001–2002, N = 11,039; 2003–2004, N = 10,122; 2005–2006, N = 10,348; 2007–2008, N = 10,149; 2009–2010, N = 10,537; 2011–2012, N = 9756; 2013–2014, N = 10,175; 2015–2016, N = 9971; 2017–2018, N = 9254). These cycles were selected on the basis of 2 critical criteria: the availability of both serum albumin and RDW measurements in all selected cycles, and the availability of mortality follow-up data through December 31, 2019, with mortality ascertainment through the National Death Index. This selection ensures complete data for the primary exposure variables (RDW and serum albumin) while allowing for comprehensive mortality outcome assessment using the most recently available death certificate linkage data. For the present analysis, we included participants who were 60 years or older at baseline and who had available measurements of RDW, serum albumin, and linked mortality data. Among 19,087 participants aged ≥60 years, those with incomplete serum albumin or RDW measurements (n = 2507) and those with missing mortality data (n = 22) were excluded, leaving 16,558 participants in the final analytic sample (Fig. 1).

Flowchart of participant selection in the study of RAR and mortality risk in older adults from NHANES (1999–2018). The flowchart presents the selection process for the final analytical cohort. Initially, the cohort comprised 101,316 participants from the NHANES (1999–2018). From 101,316 NHANES participants (1999–2018), individuals aged ≥60 years (N = 19,087) were selected. Exclusions were made for missing RAR data (N = 2507) and missing survival status (N = 22), resulting in a final sample of 16,558. RAR is defined as the ratio of RDW to serum albumin concentration. NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio, RDW = red cell distribution width.
2.2. Measurement of RAR

The exposure variable was the RAR, defined as RDW divided by serum albumin concentration. From NHANES participants, the biological samples collected for measurement of the main exposure variables included: serum, obtained from venous blood collection, used for quantification of serum albumin (g/dL); and whole blood, used for determination of RDW (%) as part of the complete blood cell count. Serum albumin was quantified using the bromocresol purple technique, and RDW was determined through automated analysis of peripheral blood samples using a Coulter analyzer. Detailed information on measurement protocols is publicly available on the NHANES website (https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/).
2.3. Determination of mortality

The primary outcome variables were all-cause mortality and CVD. All-cause mortality included deaths attributed to CVDs, malignant neoplasms, and other causes. CVD mortality was defined as deaths assigned International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision codes I00 to I09, I11, I13, I20 to I51 (cardiac diseases), and I60 to I69 (cerebrovascular diseases). Mortality data were obtained from the National Center for Health Statistics by matching NHANES mortality files with death certificate records in the National Death Index. Participants with no recorded death from the date of survey participation through December 31, 2019, were considered alive at the end of the follow-up period.
2.4. Covariates

Covariates were selected based on prior literature identifying risk factors for mortality in older adults. The fully adjusted model incorporated both continuous and categorical variables. Continuous variables included poverty-to-income ratio (PIR), body mass index (BMI), estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR; calculated using the Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology Collaboration equation), triglycerides (mg/dL), total cholesterol (mg/dL), uric acid (mg/dL), physical activity (measured in metabolic equivalent [MET]-minutes per week), and age.

Categorical variables included race/ethnicity (Hispanic [inclusive of Mexican American and other Hispanic groups], non-Hispanic White, non-Hispanic Black, and other [including Asian and mixed races]), educational attainment (less than secondary school, secondary school or equivalent, and postsecondary education), and sex. Lifestyle factors included smoking status (yes or no; participants were classified as smokers if they had smoked more than 100 cigarettes in their lifetime) and alcohol consumption (yes or no; classified as positive if more than 12 alcoholic beverages were consumed in the previous month).

Clinical conditions included diabetes mellitus (yes or no; defined as self-reported history of diabetes, fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, glycated hemoglobin level ≥6.5%, or use of insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents), hypertension (yes or no; defined as self-reported history of hypertension, average systolic blood pressure ≥140 mm Hg, average diastolic blood pressure ≥90 mm Hg, or use of antihypertensive medication), coronary heart disease (CHD; yes or no; defined as a positive response to any of the following: history of angina, myocardial infarction, or physician-diagnosed CHD), cancer (yes or no), and cerebrovascular disease (stroke; yes or no).

Biological specimens collected from NHANES participants for covariate analysis comprised: serum, utilized for the quantification of triglycerides, total cholesterol, and serum uric acid; and fasting plasma, obtained via venipuncture, employed for the measurement of fasting plasma glucose and glycated hemoglobin to assess diabetes status.
2.5. Statistical analysis

The RAR was categorized into quartiles. Categorical variables are summarized as frequencies and percentages, while continuous variables are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. Comparisons across RAR quartiles were conducted using weighted chi-squared tests for categorical variables and weighted linear regression models for continuous variables.

Cox proportional hazards (CPH) models were utilized to estimate hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the association between RAR and mortality risk. Three progressively adjusted models were constructed: Model 1 included no covariate adjustment; Model 2 adjusted for age, sex, race/ethnicity, education, smoking status, alcohol consumption, and PIR; and Model 3 included further adjustments for CHD, diabetes, hypertension, cerebrovascular disease (stroke), cancer, BMI, physical activity, eGFR, uric acid, triglycerides, and cholesterol. These 3 models were specified to evaluate the robustness of the association between RAR and mortality under progressive control for potential confounders. Model 1 provides the crude association, Model 2 accounts for sociodemographic and lifestyle factors that may influence both RAR and mortality risk, and Model 3 further adjusts for clinical comorbidities and cardiometabolic risk factors that could mediate or confound this relationship.

To evaluate whether the study was adequately powered, we conducted a post hoc power analysis for the primary associations between RAR and all-cause and CVD mortality. Using the observed HRs (2.15 for all-cause mortality and 2.05 for CVD mortality), the overall incidence of all-cause mortality, a two-sided α level of 0.001, and a target power of 90%, we calculated the minimum required sample size based on a CPH model. The achieved sample size (N = 16,558) was then compared with this minimum requirement.

Kaplan–Meier survival curves were used to visualize survival probabilities for all-cause and CVD mortality outcomes stratified by RAR quartiles. Event time was defined as the interval from baseline NHANES examination to December 31, 2019 (median follow-up 9.4 years). The log-rank tests were used to assess statistical differences among survival curves across RAR quartiles, and a P value <.05 was considered statistically significant. In addition, RAR was included as a categorical variable (quartiles) in CPH models, with the lowest quartile serving as the reference group. HR and 95% CI were reported.

To further evaluate the potential nonlinear relationship between RAR and mortality outcomes, we employed a two-stage analytical approach. First, smooth curve fitting was performed using generalized additive CPH models with penalized cubic splines to visually assess the presence of nonlinearity and preliminarily identify the inflection point region. Upon confirmation of nonlinearity, a segmented CPH regression model was used with a recursive algorithm to systematically test different cutoff values and identify the optimal threshold that maximized model likelihood. Associations between RAR and mortality on either side of the identified threshold were then examined using CPH models with univariate and multivariable adjustment. Log-likelihood ratio tests were used to compare the segmented and linear Cox models to assess the statistical significance of the identified threshold effect.

To evaluate the robustness of the main findings, we adopted a stepwise sensitivity analysis strategy. First, we used CPH models to separately assess the association between RAR and all-cause mortality in the original dataset (before missing-data imputation) and in the complete dataset (after missing-data imputation). Subsequently, subgroup analyses were conducted in the complete dataset (after missing-data imputation), covering multiple demographic and clinical subgroups, including age (≤70 years or >70 years), sex, race/ethnicity, BMI categories, physical activity levels, and the presence of comorbidities (stroke, CHD, hypertension, and diabetes). Within each subgroup, CPH models were used to evaluate the association between RAR and mortality, adjusting for all covariates except the stratifying covariate.

For continuous covariates with a missing rate >1%, we created binary indicator variables to flag missingness. For example, for BMI, we generated a binary variable G: G = 1 indicates missing BMI, and G = 0 indicates non-missing BMI, with missing values in the original continuous BMI variable imputed as 0. Both the indicator variable and the original BMI variable were included in all multivariable models along with the other covariates. For categorical variables, missing values were treated as a separate category in the regression model; for instance, BMI was divided into 3 groups: <25, ≥25, and missing.

All analyses were performed using R software (http://www.R-project.org; The R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) and Empower (R) (www.empowerstats.com; X&Y Solutions, Inc., Boston). Two-sided P values <.05 were considered statistically significant.
3. Results
3.1. Participant selection

We report that a total of 19,087 participants aged ≥60 years (median follow-up 9.4 years) initially met the preliminary eligibility criteria, of whom 2529 were excluded because they did not meet the inclusion criteria or had missing key data, leaving a final analytic sample of 16,558 participants. There were no substantial differences in key indicators (sex, age, race, education, comorbidities, etc) between the final analytic sample and those excluded due to missing data, suggesting that the final analytic cohort is largely representative of the target older adult population in the U.S. (Table S1, Supplemental Digital Content, https://links.lww.com/MD/R761).
3.2. Participant characteristics

Among the 16,558 participants (mean age 70.7 ± 7.3 years; 49.8% men), baseline characteristics differed significantly across RAR quartiles (Q1: 2.32–2.95, Q2: 2.95–3.16, Q3: 3.16–3.45, Q4: >3.45). The baseline characteristics of participants are summarized in Table 1.
Baseline characteristics of participants stratified by RAR quartiles in U.S. community-dwelling older adults from NHANES 1999 to 2018.

passages.text: The expression of continuous variables is in the form of mean ± standard deviation, with P values coming from a weighted linear regression model. Categorical variables are indicated by numbers and percentages, with P values derived from a weighted chi-squared test. The American Physical Activity Guideline defines 3 levels of physical activity: not active (0 MET-min/week), underactive (1–599 MET-min/week), and sufficiently active (600 MET-min/week or more). When over 1% of continuous variable data was missing, dummy variables were utilized to signify the absence of covariate values. When the variable was categorical, we treated it as such. Among the 16,558 patients, the amount of missing values for the covariates were 45 (0.27%) for education, 1681 (10.1%) for poverty-to-income ratio, 5576 (33.6%) for drinking, 25 (0.15%) for smoking, 6041 (36.3%) for physical activity, 487 (2.94%) for BMI, 10 (0.06%) for CHD, 3 (0.02%) for hypertension, 39 (0.23%) for stroke, 22 (0.13%) for cancer, 3398 (20.5%) for eGFR, 7 (0.04%) for cholesterol, 7 (0.04%) for uric acid, and 11 (0.07%) for triglycerides.

BMI = body mass index, CHD = coronary heart disease, eGFR = estimated glomerular filtration rate, MET = metabolic equivalent, NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio, RDW = red cell distribution width.

Demographic and clinical characteristics: with increasing RAR quartiles, age gradually increased, the proportion of participants aged ≥70 years was higher, and the proportion of men decreased. The study population consisted of Hispanic (22.6%), non-Hispanic White (52.0%), non-Hispanic Black (18.9%), and Other races (6.5%), and the proportion of non-Hispanic Black participants showed an upward trend across the quartiles. Educational attainment and the PIR ratio (mean 2.50 ± 1.54) both showed a decreasing trend in the higher RAR quartiles. Lifestyle and clinical factors: smoking status did not differ significantly across quartiles (51.6% smokers, P > .05), whereas 45.9% participants had a physical activity level of more than 600 MET-min/week, and this proportion gradually decreased with increasing RAR quartiles. The prevalence of diabetes (29.5%), hypertension (71.2%), CHD (16.3%), stroke (8.4%), and cancer (19.3%) all increased significantly in the higher RAR quartiles. Metabolic and laboratory parameters: BMI (28.9 ± 6.0 kg/m2), total cholesterol (196.1 ± 44.1 mg/dL), triglycerides (154.9 ± 105.9 mg/dL), and uric acid (5.7 ± 1.5 mg/dL) increased with higher RAR quartiles, whereas eGFR (78.2 ± 23.5 mL/min/1.73 m2) decreased progressively. RDW (13.4 ± 1.3%) increased, and serum albumin (4.2 ± 0.3 g/dL) decreased with higher RAR, which is consistent with how RAR is calculated. Except for smoking status, all characteristics differed significantly across RAR quartiles (all P < .0001).
3.3. Correlation between RAR and mortality

As presented in Table 2, higher RAR values were associated with an increased risk of both all-cause and CVD mortality among older adults. After adjustment for multiple covariates, each 1-unit increase in RAR corresponded to a 115% higher risk of all-cause mortality and a 105% higher risk of CVD mortality. Post hoc power analysis indicated that the available sample (N = 16,558; 6119 all-cause deaths; 2048 CVD deaths) provided adequate statistical power to detect the observed associations between RAR and mortality outcomes (Table S2, Supplemental Digital Content, https://links.lww.com/MD/R761).
Association between RAR and all-cause and CVD mortality in U.S. community-dwelling older adults from NHANES 1999 to 2018: Cox proportional hazards models (median follow-up: 9.4 years).

passages.text: Model 1: without adjustments. Model 2: adjustments have been implemented for sex, race, education, drinking, smoking, age, and PIR. Model 3: adjustments have been implemented for sex, race, education, drinking, smoking, CHD, stroke, cancer, diabetes, hypertension, age, PIR, eGFR, uric acid, triglycerides, cholesterol, BMI, and physical activity.

BMI = body mass index, CHD = coronary heart disease, CI = confidence interval, CVD = cardiovascular disease, eGFR = estimated glomerular filtration rate, HR = hazard ratio, NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, PIR = poverty-to-income ratio, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio.

The Kaplan–Meier curves (Fig. 2) showed a stepwise decline in survival probability among older adults with increasing RAR quartiles, and participants in the highest quartile (Q4) had the lowest survival probability throughout the follow-up period (log-rank test P < .05).

Kaplan–Meier survival curves for all-cause and CVD mortality stratified by RAR quartiles in older adults from NHANES (1999–2018). Kaplan–Meier survival curves show the cumulative survival probability by RAR quartiles (Q1–Q4) for 16,558 adults aged ≥60 years from NHANES (median follow-up: 9.4 years). The log-rank test assessed survival differences. Left panel shows all-cause mortality, and right panel shows CVD mortality. Participants in the highest RAR quartile had significantly lower survival probabilities than those in the lowest quartile for both outcomes (log-rank P < .05). CVD = cardiovascular disease, NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio, RDW = red cell distribution width.

Quartile-based analysis demonstrated that participants in the highest RAR quartile had significantly greater risks of all-cause and CVD mortality compared with those in the lowest quartile, whereas no significant differences were observed between quartiles Q2 or Q3 relative to Q1. Trend analysis confirmed a positive dose–response relationship between increasing RAR levels and both all-cause and CVD mortality.
3.4. Nonlinear association between RAR and mortality

The results of smooth curve fitting showed that there were nonlinear relationships between RAR and both all-cause mortality and CVD mortality (Fig. 3). This nonlinear association remained consistent before and after handling missing data.

Dose–response relationship between RAR and all-cause and CVD mortality in older adults from NHANES (1999–2018). Generalized additive models assessed the nonlinear link between RAR and mortality risk in adults aged ≥60 years (P < .001). The solid red line represents the estimated HR, and the dashed lines represent the 95% CI. Left panel displays all-cause mortality, and right panel shows CVD mortality. Adjustments were made for sex, race, education, alcohol, smoking, CHD, stroke, cancer, diabetes, hypertension, age, PIR, eGFR, uric acid, triglycerides, total cholesterol, BMI, and physical activity. BMI = body mass index, CHD = coronary heart disease, CI = confidence interval, CVD = cardiovascular disease, eGFR = estimated glomerular filtration rate, HR = hazard ratio, NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, PIR = poverty-to-income ratio, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio.

Threshold effect analysis identified an inflection point at RAR = 4.05 (Table 3). When RAR was below the inflection point (<4.05), higher values were significantly associated with increased risks of all-cause mortality (HR = 2.51, 95% CI = 2.04–3.10, P < .0001) and CVD mortality (HR = 2.65, 95% CI = 1.84–3.83, P < .0001). When RAR was greater than or equal to the inflection point (≥4.05), the magnitude of the association with all-cause mortality was attenuated but remained statistically significant. In contrast, the association with CVD mortality was no longer statistically significant, indicating a saturation effect at higher RAR levels.

Threshold effect analysis of RAR and all-cause and CVD mortality in U.S. community-dwelling older adults from NHANES 1999 to 2018: two-piecewise Cox proportional hazards models (median follow-up: 9.4 years).

BMI = body mass index, CHD = coronary heart disease, CI = confidence interval, CVD = cardiovascular disease, eGFR = estimated glomerular filtration rate, HR = hazard ratio, NHANES = National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey, PIR = poverty-to-income ratio, RAR = red cell distribution width-to-albumin ratio.

Log-likelihood ratio testing yielded P values <.001, indicating that the nonlinear model with an inflection point provided a significantly better fit than a linear model, supporting the presence of a threshold effect in the relationship between RAR and mortality.
3.5. Stratified and sensitivity analyses

To assess the robustness of the findings, we used CPH models to compare analyses before and after handling missing data, and found that the association between RAR and all-cause mortality was consistent with the main analysis (Table 4). Using the dataset after missing-data handling, we then performed stratified analyses by age, sex, alcohol consumption, smoking status, physical activity, comorbidities, and other covariates. We found that higher RAR values remained significantly associated with increased all-cause mortality across these subgroups (Fig. 4).
Association between RAR and all-cause mortality before and after missing data imputation in U.S. community-dwelling older adults from NHANES 1999 to 2018 (median follow-up: 9.4 years).

4. Discussion

This study, based on nationally representative data from the 1999 to 2018 NHANES, is the first cohort investigation to systematically examine the association between the RAR and long-term mortality among community-dwelling older adults (aged ≥60 years; median follow-up 9.4 years). Although participants with missing key information were excluded, the final analytic cohort remained broadly representative of the target older population. Among 16,558 older adults with follow-up through December 31, 2019, a total of 6119 deaths occurred (median follow-up 9.4 years). Higher RAR quartiles were associated with a more adverse baseline risk profile, including older age, lower socioeconomic status, reduced physical activity, and a greater burden of cardiometabolic comorbidities. RAR showed a strong, independent, and dose-dependentpositive association with both all-cause (HR = 2.15, 95% CI = 1.89–2.45) and CVD mortality (HR = 2.05, 95% CI = 1.74–2.41). Notably, this relationship was distinctly nonlinear, with a critical threshold at RAR = 4.05: below this value, mortality risk rose steeply, whereas above it the association attenuated and CVD mortality approached a plateau, representing a saturation phenomenon not previously reported. These associations remained robust across multiple sensitivity and subgroup analyses, underscoring the potential value of RAR as a simple, low-cost marker for long-term mortality risk stratification in high-risk older adults, in whom the prognostic impact of inflammation and nutritional imbalance may differ substantially from that in younger or mixed-age populations.

Baseline characteristics of the study population differed systematically across RAR quartiles, demonstrating a clear pattern of increasing health burden with rising RAR levels. Specifically, participants with higher RAR values were older, of lower socioeconomic status, less physically active, and carried substantially greater burdens of cardiometabolic comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension, CHD, stroke) and metabolic dysfunction (elevated BMI, triglycerides, uric acid; reduced eGFR). The progressive accumulation of these unfavorable characteristics across RAR quartiles reveals that RAR is not merely a laboratory artifact but an integrated biomarker that captures real, clinically meaningful differences in overall health status and disease burden. This characteristic pattern supports the biological plausibility of RAR’s independent association with mortality, reflecting its capacity to synthesize multiple health risk dimensions into a single measure. In older adults, elevated RAR reflects the synergistic burden of age-related physiological decline, systemic inflammation, and nutritional compromise that drives mortality risk.

Our findings demonstrate that RAR independently predicts all-cause and CVD mortality in community-dwelling older adults with a significant dose–response relationship (HR = 2.15, 95% CI = 1.89–2.45), consistent with recent large-scale cohort studies. Hao et al analyzed 469,572 individuals from UK Biobank and NHANES, reporting comparable effect sizes (HR = 1.82–2.20), confirming cross-cohort consistency. However, unlike prior studies examining broader age ranges (UK: 56.6 ± 8.1; NHANES: 48.6 ± 18.7 years) or disease-specific populations, this study uniquely focused on older adults (mean age 70.7 ± 7.3 years; n = 16,558; median follow-up 9.4 years). This population exhibited substantially higher chronic disease prevalence than mixed-age cohorts. The elevated disease burden likely amplifies RAR’s prognostic significance, further substantiated by disease-specific evidence: Liu et al demonstrated that RAR ≥ 3.22 was associated with increased mortality in diabetic patients; Zhao et al reported 88% and 115% increases in 30-day and 1-year mortality per unit RAR increase in acute stroke patients. These findings establish RAR as a more potent mortality predictor in high-disease-burden populations. These observations underscore the importance of methodological rigor. Key strengths of this study include: methodologically, this investigation’s strengths include: exclusive older adult focus eliminating age-related confounding; extended 9.4-year follow-up capturing long-term outcomes; large nationally representative sample ensuring generalizability; and comprehensive covariate adjustment accounting for demographic, lifestyle, and clinical factors. Biologically, RAR’s enhanced prognostic significance in older adults likely reflects the prevalence of chronic low-grade inflammation (driven by immunosenescence and senescent cell accumulation) and nutritional imbalance (declining albumin synthesis and reduced protein intake) – 2 synergistic biological processes uniquely captured by this accessible biomarker. These findings establish RAR as a valuable risk stratification tool for identifying high-risk patients in primary care settings serving older adults.

A new finding is the clear nonlinear relationship between RAR and mortality. A piecewise Cox regression model identified using a recursive algorithm confirmed an inflection point at RAR = 4.05, and a log-likelihood ratio test showed that the nonlinear threshold model provided a better fit than the linear model (P < .001). Below this threshold, the risk of death rises sharply; above the threshold, the association weakens. This threshold is close to the value observed by Huang et al in a hypertensive population (RAR = 4.10), where they also found that the association between RAR and mortality diminished at higher values. However, in this older cohort, our study uniquely identified a previously unreported phenomenon: above the threshold, all-cause mortality remains associated with RAR (though attenuated), whereas the association with CVD mortality loses statistical significance and reaches a plateau. This divergence reveals a critical mechanistic shift. At lower RAR values, inflammation–malnutrition manifests primarily as cardiovascular dysfunction, making RAR a strong predictor of CVD. At higher RAR values, severe systemic inflammation and nutritional depletion trigger widespread multisystem decompensation. Consequently, deaths increasingly result from competing causes – such as infections, malignancies, sepsis, renal failure, or respiratory failure – rather than from cardiovascular events alone. These competing risks dilute the CVD signal while preserving the signal for all-cause mortality. This elderly-specific pattern of CVD saturation is reported here for the first time in the RAR literature and fundamentally reshapes our understanding of the prognostic value of RAR in older adults. It suggests that management strategies for older individuals with high RAR should focus on multisystem dysfunction and competing mortality risks, rather than solely on cardiovascular prevention.

To rigorously evaluate result robustness, CPH models comparing RAR-mortality associations before and after missing data imputation yielded consistent results. Stratified analyses across demographic and clinical dimensions – age (≤70 vs >70 years), sex, race/ethnicity, BMI categories, physical activity levels, and comorbidities (stroke, CHD, hypertension, diabetes) – demonstrated that higher RAR values remained significantly associated with increased all-cause mortality across all subgroups (Fig. 4), without meaningful effect modification. The concordance of findings across diverse analytical approaches indicates that the observed RAR-mortality association reflects a genuine biological relationship rather than analytical artifacts or residual confounding. These comprehensive sensitivity and stratified analyses establish RAR’s prognostic reliability across heterogeneous older adult populations, demonstrating that RAR’s predictive capacity for mortality risk is robust and generalizable regardless of demographic or clinical characteristics, thereby confirming RAR as a dependable mortality risk marker in community-dwelling older adults.

The biological mechanisms underlying the association between elevated RAR and increased mortality risk in older adults are multifactorial. RAR, calculated as the ratio of RDW to serum albumin, reflects systemic inflammation and nutritional status. Chronic inflammation in older adults activates signaling pathways, including JAK/STAT, PI3K/AKT, and Ras/MAPK, via erythropoietin receptor interference, while also inducing inhibitory molecules such as cytokine-inducible SH2-containing protein. This process disrupts core erythropoietin receptor signaling, thereby reducing erythroid cell responsiveness to erythropoietin. In addition, pro-inflammatory mediators impair hepcidin regulation, leading to functionally iron-deficient red blood cells. These mechanisms collectively increase the proportion of immature or morphologically abnormal erythrocytes, contributing to elevated RDW.

RDW reflects variability in erythrocyte size, and its elevation adversely affects erythrocyte function by impairing oxygen transport efficiency, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity, and promoting microthrombosis. These alterations contribute to inadequate tissue perfusion and hypoxia, thereby exacerbating tissue damage and increasing the risk of multi-organ dysfunction. Previous studies have demonstrated associations between elevated RDW and multiple disease states as well as adverse clinical outcomes.

Furthermore, chronic inflammation can compromise vascular endothelial integrity through activation of the NF-kB signaling pathway, resulting in increased vascular permeability and reduced serum albumin levels. Concurrently, older adults frequently experience nutritional and metabolic imbalances, which further exacerbate the development of hypoalbuminemia and contribute to elevated RAR levels. Hypoalbuminemia has been associated with functional decline, disease progression, and increased mortality risk.

Taken together, elevated RDW and decreased albumin levels act synergistically to heighten mortality risk in older adults. Thus, RAR may serve as a valuable marker for mortality risk in this demographic, reflecting the interplay of various pathological mechanisms associated with inflammation and nutritional imbalance.

This study has several strengths. First, it employed rigorous participant selection criteria and extensive validation to ensure the representativeness of the final cohort. Among 19,087 community-dwelling older adults (≥60 years) identified from NHANES 1999 to 2018, we systematically excluded participants with incomplete RAR data (n = 2507) or missing mortality follow-up information (n = 22), resulting in a final analytic sample of 16,558 participants. Importantly, our analysis demonstrated that there were no substantial differences in key demographic and clinical indicators – including sex, age, race, education, and comorbidities – between the final analytic sample and those excluded due to missing data. This finding validates that the final cohort is largely representative of the target U.S. population of community-dwelling older adults and minimizes selection bias. Second, the study utilized the nationally representative NHANES database, which enrolls civilian, noninstitutionalized U.S. residents from all 50 states and the District of Columbia. The NHANES cohort encompasses a diverse older adult population across multiple demographic and socioeconomic strata, and its standardized, scientifically rigorous data collection procedures – including standardized laboratory measurements and linkage with the National Death Index for mortality ascertainment – enhance both the external validity and reliability of the findings. Third, the robustness of the primary results was further validated through stratified analyses across multiple demographic and clinical subgroups (age, sex, race/ethnicity, BMI, physical activity, and comorbidities) and sensitivity analyses comparing results before and after handling missing data.

However, some limitations should be acknowledged. Although the study population consisted of U.S. adults aged 60 years and older and was nationally representative, the generalizability of these findings to other regions or ethnic groups requires further validation. Second, as a retrospective observational design, causality cannot be inferred, and residual confounding from unmeasured variables is possible despite multivariable adjustment. Third, RAR was assessed at a single time point; changes over time were not considered, which may have introduced time-related bias. Given these limitations, future prospective studies involving diverse populations and repeated RAR measurements are warranted to validate and expand upon these findings.
5. Conclusion

In conclusion, this large prospective cohort study demonstrates that the RAR is an independent and nonlinear predictor of all-cause and CVD mortality in community-dwelling older adults, with a critical threshold at RAR = 4.05 that reveals distinct mechanistic patterns of mortality risk. The pronounced nonlinearity – characterized by steep risk escalation below the threshold and a plateau effect above it, with CVD mortality demonstrating saturation – underscores that RAR captures not merely cardiovascular dysfunction but also multisystem decompensation in advanced aging. As an accessible, low-cost biomarker derived from routine laboratory measurements, RAR offers substantial clinical utility for mortality risk stratification in primary care and community-based settings serving older populations. These findings support the integration of RAR into population-level screening algorithms to identify high-risk older adults warranting intensive preventive and therapeutic interventions.
